threats.title: Arbitrary Execution of Defeated and Expired Governance Proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: The governance execute() function allows any proposal that has finished its voting period to be executed, regardless of whether it passed, failed, or expired.
threats.investigation_rationale: The require statement in execute() explicitly permits ProposalState.Defeated and ProposalState.Expired. This completely bypasses the voting consensus mechanism, allowing an attacker to propose malicious actions (e.g., draining the treasury) and execute them even if 100% of voters vote against them.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(status == ProposalState.Succeeded || status == ProposalState.Defeated || status == ProposalState.Expired)

preconditions: Attacker has enough voting power to submit a proposal
Voting period for the malicious proposal has ended

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: status == ProposalState.Defeated is accepted as valid for execution
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker submits proposal to transfer protocol funds to themselves, waits for it to be defeated, then executes it
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts all value controlled by the governance timelock/executor

economic_questions: What assets and permissions are controlled by the Governor contract?
What is the proposal threshold required to submit a proposal?
validation_requirements: Verify if the Governor contract is the owner of critical protocol components
Test executing a defeated proposal in a forked environment

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Standard gas costs for proposal creation and execution
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Total Value Locked (TVL) controlled by governance

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after the voting period ends
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol insolvency and complete loss of trust

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All protocol users and token holders

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm state() returns Defeated correctly and execute() accepts it

threats.title: Fake Genesis Pool Injection Allows Arbitrary Gauge Creation and Token Whitelisting
threats.hypothesis_description: GenesisPoolManager.depositToken() accepts any address as a genesis pool without verifying it was created by the GenesisPoolFactory, allowing attackers to inject malicious pool contracts.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because the manager trusts the boolean return value and the data returned by getLiquidityPoolInfo() from the provided address, an attacker can trigger _preLaunchPool(). This calls gaugeManager.createGauge() for an arbitrary pair and whitelists an arbitrary token, potentially redirecting protocol emissions to the attacker's pair.
preconditions: Attacker deploys a custom contract implementing IGenesisPool
Attacker calls GenesisPoolManager.depositToken with the custom contract address

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No factory validation: genesisFactory.getGenesisPool(nativeToken) == genesisPool is not checked
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker forces the creation of a gauge for a pair they fully control
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker captures protocol emissions (Black tokens) directed to the newly created malicious gauge

economic_questions: Can the attacker vote for their malicious gauge to direct emissions?
Does gauge creation automatically grant emissions without governance approval?
validation_requirements: Verify if gaugeManager.createGauge() has any internal whitelisting checks
Test the injection of a fake pool in a local fork

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (gas for contract deployment and transaction)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on voting power directed to the malicious gauge

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Dilution of emissions for legitimate pools

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Reduced yield due to emission diversion

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Validate that depositToken() can be called by anyone

threats.title: Permanent Lock of Token Owner LP Shares via Underflow in deductAmount
threats.hypothesis_description: When the token owner attempts to unstake LP tokens from the gauge, the deductAmount callback calculates a deduction from userDeposits. If the owner did not make a normal user deposit, this subtraction underflows, permanently locking their LP tokens.
threats.investigation_rationale: The logic in deductAmount subtracts a calculated userAmount from userDeposits[account]. For the tokenOwner, if gaugeTokenAmount >= pendingOwnerStaked, it adjusts userAmount but still subtracts it from userDeposits[tokenOwner]. If the owner only provided native tokens and no funding tokens, userDeposits[tokenOwner] is 0, causing a guaranteed revert.

proof_of_signal.pattern: userDeposits[account] -= userAmount; where userDeposits[account] can be 0 for tokenOwner

preconditions: Genesis pool has launched and LP tokens are staked in the gauge
Token owner attempts to unstake an amount >= pendingOwnerStaked
Token owner has 0 userDeposits (did not deposit funding tokens)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: userDeposits[account] -= userAmount without checking if userDeposits >= userAmount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Normal protocol operation triggers an arithmetic underflow
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit for attacker, but massive financial loss (locked funds) for the token owner

economic_questions: What is the value of the locked LP tokens?
Is there any alternative way for the token owner to recover their share?
validation_requirements: Simulate token owner unstaking in a local fork
Verify if deductAllAmount suffers from the same issue (it does not, it sets to 0)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: None (self-inflicted or triggered by normal use)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When token owner tries to unstake
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of liquidity for the token owner

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: 50% of the total liquidity generated by the genesis pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check gauge unstake flow calling deductAmount
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Calculate exact userAmount when gaugeTokenAmount >= pendingOwnerStaked

threats.title: Governance Router Update Bricked via Inverted Zero-Address Check
threats.hypothesis_description: The setRouter function in GenesisPoolManager requires the new router address to be the zero address, making it impossible to update the router to a valid contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: The require statement is `require(_router == address(0), "ZA");` instead of `!=`. If the owner attempts to legitimately upgrade the router, the transaction reverts. If they mistakenly pass address(0), it succeeds, permanently breaking the launch() function for all future genesis pools because they will attempt to call addLiquidity on the zero address.

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Equality operator used instead of inequality for zero-address validation
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Owner accidentally sets router to address(0), bricking protocol
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes complete failure of the genesis pool launch mechanism

economic_questions: Can the protocol function if the router needs to be upgraded?
Are there any fallback mechanisms for launching pools?
validation_requirements: Verify the exact syntax in the deployed contract
Check if the router is set correctly during initialize()

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Protocol halt for new genesis pools

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Protocol and future genesis pool creators

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check initialize() to ensure initial router is set correctly

threats.title: Quorum Manipulation via Current Total Supply Evaluation
threats.hypothesis_description: The quorum calculation in L2GovernorVotesQuorumFraction uses the current total supply of smNFTs rather than the historical supply at the proposal snapshot, allowing dynamic manipulation of the quorum threshold.
threats.investigation_rationale: The quorum() function ignores the blockTimestamp parameter and calls token.getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() without a timestamp. This means an attacker can mint or burn tokens while a proposal is active to artificially raise or lower the quorum requirement, ensuring malicious proposals pass or legitimate ones fail.
preconditions: Attacker has the ability to influence the total supply of smNFTs (e.g., via minting, burning, or locking/unlocking mechanics)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: getsmNFTPastTotalSupply() is called without the blockTimestamp parameter
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker increases total supply right before voting ends to raise quorum above the current vote count
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can block unfavorable governance actions or force through favorable ones

economic_questions: How easily can the total supply of smNFTs be changed?
What is the capital cost to mint/burn enough tokens to swing the quorum?
validation_requirements: Analyze the IBlackHoleVotes token contract to determine supply elasticity
Calculate the cost of quorum manipulation

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on the cost to mint smNFTs
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of the governance proposal outcome

threats.title: Minority Veto via Abstain Votes in Governance
threats.hypothesis_description: The _voteSucceeded logic requires forVotes to be strictly greater than abstainVotes. This allows a minority of voters to block a proposal by abstaining, even if the vast majority of active votes are in favor.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard governance models use abstain votes only to reach quorum, not to oppose a proposal. By requiring forVotes > abstainVotes, an attacker with a minority stake can simply vote Abstain instead of Against to effectively double their blocking power (since it raises the bar for forVotes without contributing to againstVotes).

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker casts Abstain votes to prevent a proposal from succeeding without needing to outvote the For votes directly
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker can extort the protocol or block unfavorable parameter changes with less capital than normally required

economic_questions: What is the exact voting power required to block a proposal using this method?
How does this interact with the execute() vulnerability?
validation_requirements: Simulate a vote where For > Against but Abstain > For
Verify the resulting state is Expired

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Enough voting power to exceed the For votes
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect value of blocking a proposal

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Review voting dynamics and quorum requirements
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm the state transitions when Abstain > For

threats.title: Multi-Block Sandwich Attack on Genesis Pool Launch Liquidity
threats.hypothesis_description: The GenesisPool adds liquidity to the DEX with zero slippage protection (amountAMin=0, amountBMin=0). Because the launch is triggered predictably by an automated keeper at the epoch flip, an attacker can manipulate the pool reserves in a preceding block and extract value.
threats.investigation_rationale: Although there is no public mempool for same-block front-running, the predictability of the checkAtEpochFlip execution allows a multi-block MEV attack. The attacker skews the pair ratio in block N, the keeper executes the launch in block N+1 (adding liquidity at the skewed ratio), and the attacker back-runs in block N+2 to extract the arbitrage profit.
preconditions: The pair must already have some initial liquidity (or attacker provides it)
The epoch flip execution time is predictable (e.g., via Chainlink Automation)
Attacker can reliably land transactions in adjacent blocks

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amountAMin and amountBMin are hardcoded to 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker skews the AMM reserves, forcing the GenesisPool to deposit at a bad price, then reverses the trade
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts a portion of the Genesis Pool's native and funding tokens as arbitrage profit

economic_questions: Is the pair guaranteed to be empty before launch?
Does pairFactory.setGenesisStatus prevent trading before launch?
validation_requirements: Check if setGenesisStatus(true) blocks swaps on the pair
Simulate a multi-block sandwich attack with a Chainlink keeper

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Sufficient capital to move the AMM price significantly
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 10-20% of the Genesis Pool TVL depending on pool depth
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI on gas and swap fees

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: High capital to manipulate the pool
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Genesis pool depositors receive significantly fewer LP tokens

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Genesis pool depositors and token owner
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional loss of LP token value
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total native and funding tokens in the pool

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Calculate exact slippage loss with 0 min amounts

threats.title: Genesis Pool Bricked via Auction Fallback Removal
threats.hypothesis_description: If the default auction at index 0 is removed from the AuctionFactory, any new Genesis Pool created with an invalid auctionIndex will silently fall back to address(0), permanently bricking the pool's deposit functionality.
threats.investigation_rationale: In depositNativeToken, if the requested auctionIndex returns address(0), it falls back to auctions(0). If auctions(0) has been removed (set to address(0) via removeAuction), the Genesis Pool is initialized with an auction address of 0x0. Subsequent calls to depositToken will attempt to call getNativeTokenAmount on address(0), causing a revert and locking the token owner's native tokens.
preconditions: Admin has removed the auction at index 0 in AuctionFactory
Token owner calls depositNativeToken with an invalid auctionIndex

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Fallback to auctions(0) without verifying it is not address(0)
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Token owner accidentally or maliciously creates a bricked pool
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes permanent loss of the proposedNativeAmount for the token owner

economic_questions: Can the token owner recover their native tokens if the pool is bricked?
Can Governance reject the pool to trigger a refund?
validation_requirements: Verify if Governance can call rejectGenesisPool to rescue the funds
Test the fallback logic when auctions(0) is address(0)

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm revert behavior on address(0) call
